title: Measure Retrieval Recall Before You Blame the LLM
linkedin_md:
# Measure Retrieval Recall Before You Blame the LLM

A lot of “LLM quality” bugs are not LLM bugs.

The model gives a vague answer. It cites the wrong section. It refuses even though the answer exists. It invents a policy that sounds plausible. The usual reaction is to tweak the prompt, swap models, add a reranker, increase `top_k`, or argue about temperature.

Sometimes that helps. Often it just hides the real issue: the model never received the right evidence.

If you are building retrieval-augmented systems, retrieval recall is the first thing to measure. Not vibes. Not answer quality. Not “it worked on my test question.” Actual recall.

Given a query, did the retrieval system return the document chunk that contains the answer?

Until you know that, you do not know whether the generator failed or the retriever failed.

[YOUR STORY HERE - 2-3 paragraphs]

## The Basic Split

A production RAG system has at least two major failure surfaces:

1. Retrieval: finding the right context.
2. Generation: using that context correctly.

If retrieval misses, the model is forced to guess, abstain, or synthesize from weak evidence. If retrieval succeeds and generation still fails, then you can look at prompt design, context formatting, instruction hierarchy, citation requirements, or model capability.

The mistake is collapsing both into one metric like “answer accepted” or “thumbs up.”

That metric is useful, but it is downstream. It cannot tell you where the system broke.

A simple rule: before debugging the answer, inspect the evidence.

## Build a Recall Eval Set

Start with 100 to 300 realistic questions.

Do not generate only clean textbook queries. Use the kinds of queries users actually send:

- Underspecified: “What is the refund window?”
- Colloquial: “Can I get my money back after two weeks?”
- Multi-hop: “What happens if I cancel after the trial but before annual renewal?”
- Entity-heavy: “Does this apply to contractors in California?”
- Negative cases: “Is phone support available on weekends?”

For each query, label the smallest source span that contains the answer. This can be a document ID, page, section, paragraph, or chunk ID. Start rough if you need to. A good enough label beats no label.

The key is that the label must be independent of the retriever. Do not label the top result after retrieval. Label from the source corpus.

Then run your retrieval pipeline and measure:

- Recall@1: did the correct chunk appear first?
- Recall@5: did it appear in the top 5?
- Recall@10: did it appear in the top 10?
- MRR: how high did the first relevant result rank?
- Empty-hit rate: how often did nothing relevant appear?
- Distractor rate: how often did a misleading but semantically similar chunk appear above the right one?

Recall@5 is often the most useful early metric because many RAG systems pass 3 to 8 chunks into the model. If the answer is not in that window, generation quality is already capped.

## Log Retrieval Like a First-Class System

Every production query should log the retrieval trace.

At minimum:

```text
query_id
user_query
normalized_query
retrieval_strategy
embedding_model_version
index_version
filters_applied
top_k
returned_chunk_ids
returned_scores
source_document_ids
reranker_version
final_context_chunk_ids
generation_model_version
answer_status
user_feedback
```

This does not require storing sensitive user content forever. You can hash identifiers, sample traffic, redact payloads, or separate raw traces from aggregate metrics. The important part is that you can reconstruct what context the model saw.

Without this, you are debugging smoke.

You also want to distinguish candidate retrieval from final context selection. A vector search may find the right chunk at rank 12, but your reranker or truncation step may drop it. That is a different bug from never retrieving it at all.

Track both:

- Candidate recall@50
- Final context recall@K

If candidate recall is high and final recall is low, fix reranking, filtering, deduping, chunk packing, or token budgeting.

If candidate recall is low, fix indexing, chunking, embeddings, query rewriting, metadata filters, or corpus coverage.

## Common Retrieval Failures

Chunking is usually the first suspect.

Chunks that are too small lose surrounding definitions and conditions. Chunks that are too large dilute the embedding. A practical starting range is 300 to 800 tokens with 10 to 20 percent overlap, but the right answer depends on the document shape.

Tables, policy docs, API references, and support articles need different chunking strategies. A paragraph splitter can destroy a table. A fixed token splitter can separate a condition from its exception.

Metadata filters are another common failure. Filters feel deterministic, so they get less scrutiny. In practice, they silently erase recall.

Examples:

- Wrong locale filter.
- Stale product version.
- Missing permission tag.
- Overly strict document type.
- Date filter excluding still-valid content.

Instrument filter impact. Log how many candidates existed before and after each filter. If a filter removes 98 percent of candidates, you should know that.

Query rewriting can also hurt. A rewrite that sounds cleaner to humans may remove the exact term needed to retrieve the right document. Measure recall before and after rewriting. Keep the original query in the retrieval mix unless you have evidence that rewrite-only wins.

## Use Answer Evals Only After Retrieval Evals

Once retrieval recall is measured, answer evaluation becomes much easier.

Split your evals into buckets:

- Retrieval missed, answer failed.
- Retrieval hit, answer failed.
- Retrieval hit, answer passed.
- Retrieval missed, answer passed.

That last bucket matters. If the answer passed without the labeled source, either your label is incomplete, your corpus has duplicate evidence, or the model guessed correctly. Do not ignore it.

For “retrieval hit, answer failed,” inspect whether the relevant chunk was visible enough. Was it buried under eight distractors? Was it truncated? Was the answer in a table serialized badly? Did the prompt ask the model to prioritize a less relevant section?

This is where prompt work actually becomes grounded.

## Set Thresholds Before You Ship

Pick operating targets by workflow risk.

For low-stakes internal search, Recall@5 above 80 percent might be acceptable if the UI shows sources and users can inspect them.

For customer-facing answers, aim higher. Recall@5 below 90 percent will create a steady stream of confident failures.

For high-impact domains, retrieval misses should trigger abstention, fallback search, or human review. Do not let the model smooth over missing evidence.

Also track regressions by slice:

- Query length.
- Document type.
- Language.
- Customer segment.
- Fresh versus old documents.
- Head queries versus long-tail queries.
- Permission-filtered queries.

Overall recall can look stable while one important slice collapses.

[YOUR STORY HERE - 2-3 paragraphs]

## Takeaways

Measure retrieval before tuning generation.

Create a labeled query set with source spans, even if it starts small.

Track candidate recall separately from final context recall.

Log retrieval traces with index versions, filters, chunk IDs, scores, and final context.

Treat filters, chunking, reranking, and query rewriting as measurable components, not implementation details.

When the model fails, ask the first question first: did it actually get the evidence?

---
**Tools I actually use**

- [Cursor](https://cursor.com) : where most of my agent-loop iteration happens
- [Beehiiv](https://www.beehiiv.com) : runs this newsletter's email list
- [Hostinger VPS](https://www.hostinger.com/vps-hosting) : the $12/mo box my agents live on
